articles.title: Home Cooks Bring Back Old British Foods
articles.summary: Some home cooks are making old British dishes that many people have forgotten. Annie Mae Herring is a food creator who tries recipes like carrageen pudding, which is a dessert made with seaweed. She also makes other foods like Bedfordshire clanger, a pasty that is sweet on one side and savory on the other. People watch her videos and remember eating these dishes when they were kids. They share stories about school dinners and family meals. Another cook, Shannon McCarthy, also explores old recipes from different parts of the UK. This helps keep these foods alive and brings back happy memories for many.

articles.title: Mortgage Rates Go Down After War Worries
articles.summary: Mortgage rates are starting to fall after going up because of a war in Iran. This is good news for people trying to buy their first home, like Amy and Tommy, who have been saving for years. They hope the rates will drop more before they buy their house. The war made things expensive, like petrol and food, which made it hard for many people. Lenders, who give money for mortgages, are cutting rates because they think the war might end soon, which could help the economy. But experts say rates could change suddenly, so it's still tricky for buyers. Amy says having a home shouldn't be a luxury and worries about others who work in jobs like supermarkets.

articles.title: A Break in Fighting Brings Hope in Lebanon
articles.summary: After weeks of fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon, a new truce has started. This means both sides have agreed to stop fighting for now. People in Lebanon are celebrating cautiously because they hope the peace will last. At the same time, Iran has reopened the Strait of Hormuz, a busy waterway for ships carrying oil. President Trump from the U.S. supports this move but says the U.S. will still block ships from going to Iranian ports. There are also talks about Iran's nuclear program and a cease-fire between the U.S. and Iran that might end soon. The article reports on these events to show how countries are trying to avoid more conflict.

articles.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz Open for Ships?
articles.summary: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway in the Middle East that is very important for ships carrying oil and goods. Recently, Iran and the U.S. said it is open, but experts like Ian Ralby explain it is not fully open. Iran is controlling the strait by making ships ask for permission and follow special routes near its coast. This means ships cannot go freely, which affects global trade. The situation is uncertain, and it is hard to know when it will be truly open again. This article helps us understand why this matters for shipping and the world.

articles.title: Senate Votes to Keep Spy Program for a Little While
articles.summary: The U.S. Senate has voted to keep a spy program called FISA running until April 30. This program helps spy agencies watch people to keep the country safe, but some people think it's not fair because it might invade privacy. At the same time, big storms are hitting the Midwest, causing damage and setting rain records. In another story, an 85-year-old woman who was held in immigration custody has gone back home. These events show how the government makes decisions and how weather can affect people's lives.

articles.title: Judge Says No to Government Asking for Voter Information
articles.summary: A federal judge in Rhode Island said no to a lawsuit from the U.S. Department of Justice that wanted personal details about voters. The judge agreed with state election officials and civil rights groups, saying the government cannot just ask for lots of private information without a good reason. The information includes things like birth dates and addresses. The government says it needs this to keep elections safe, but many people worry it could be used to check if voters are citizens or for other purposes. This has happened in other states too, where judges have also said no. Some states have given the information, but Rhode Island is protecting voter privacy.
